title: JTBD Analysis — Elementary and Secondary Schools (NAICS 611110)

sections.title: Derivation Chain
sections.content:
```
CFJ     "Deliver structured K-12 academic instruction to enrolled students
         within a safe, legally compliant, and supervised facility environment"
  |
  v  Anchor    L5.d.2 — Emergency Exit Alarm & Monitoring Systems
               parent: L6d — Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management
  |
  v  Lens      "What monitoring does Campus Safety & Emergency Management need
                to protect occupants and enable legally compliant evacuation?"
  |
  v  Product   "Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to monitor
     Job       and secure all emergency exit doors against unauthorized use
               during occupied school hours while ensuring instant single-motion
               evacuation capability per IBC / NFPA 101"
  |
  v  Alternatives   5 (wired panel, mechanical-only, wireless IoT, security patrol, do nothing)
  |
  v  Needs    24 error statements across 7 relevant job steps + P3/P4/P5
```

---

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, Offering Level

| NAICS | CFJ | Output Types | Türwächter Offering Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 611110 | Deliver structured K-12 academic instruction to enrolled students within a safe, legally compliant, and supervised facility environment | Public elementary (K–5/K–6); Public middle (6–8); Public high school (9–12); Private/parochial (K–12 subset); Charter school (K–12 subset) | L5 — system within L6d process step. Route: PROCESS_VN (product enables the school's operation, never ends up in the educational output). |

### 1.2 Segments (by circumstance)

| Segment | Defining Circumstance | Why Distinct |
|---|---|---|
| **Aging-stock district** | Average building age 49 years; no conduit to secondary exits; wiring retrofit cost-prohibitive | Wireless-only path; strong ROI on avoiding electrical work |
| **Post-Uvalde compliance mandate** | School in 32+ US states with new door-security legislation; procurement driven by law not discretion | Safety budget unlocked; compliance deadline creates urgency |
| **Multi-campus district portfolio** | 10+ schools under single facilities director; centralized monitoring is the value driver, not per-door cost | Dashboard/aggregation value dominates; per-unit economics secondary |
| **High-traffic perimeter zone** | Schools with gyms, cafeterias, or sports fields accessed via secondary exits multiple times daily | Battery life constraint (C4) dominant; false alarm management critical |
| **Private / charter school** | Independent procurement authority; can act without LEA approval cycle; often tighter budgets | Faster sales cycle; price sensitivity higher; IoT ROI must be self-evident |

*Note: Segments 1.3–1.5 (Process VN, BOM, Edges) already derived in `VN_elementary_secondary_schools.md`. NAICS 611110 is Service — no Product BOM applies.*

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Route** | PROCESS_VN (product is used in campus operations; never part of educational output) |
| **Anchor unit** | Emergency Exit Alarm & Monitoring Systems |
| **Anchor level** | L5 (standalone security system) |
| **Parent unit** | Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management (L6d) |
| **Parent job_family** | "Protect occupants and property while enabling legally compliant evacuation at all times" |
| **L4 match** | Door Handle Guard, Alarm Horn/Sounder, Door Position Sensor, Wireless Radio Transmitter, Battery Pack, Wall-Mount Receiver/Gateway — all map 1:1 to Türwächter L4 subsystems |

### 2.2 Lens

| Lens component | Value |
|---|---|
| **Function verb** | Monitor and secure (from L6d job_family: "protect occupants while enabling legally compliant evacuation") |
| **Beneficiary** | Campus safety coordinator / facilities manager acting on behalf of L6d |
| **Lens statement** | "What monitoring and securing does Campus Safety & Emergency Management need to protect occupants and remain evacuation-compliant during occupied school hours?" |

### 2.3 Product Job

```
"Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to monitor and secure
all emergency exit doors against unauthorized use during occupied school hours
while ensuring instant single-motion evacuation capability per IBC / NFPA 101
and generating auditable compliance documentation"
```

*Passes abstraction test: 3+ alternative categories exist. Technology-agnostic: survives replacement of wireless guard with wired panel or security personnel.*

### 2.4 Alternatives (5)

| Alternative | UNSPSC | Market Share at L5.d.2 | Inherent Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wired panic hardware + alarm panel integration** | 46191501 | ~55% | High installation labor; conduit to each door; retrofit disruption in 49-yr-old buildings; ongoing panel maintenance; certified integrator required |
| **Mechanical-only door alarms (no monitoring)** | 46171600 | ~30% | Zero monitoring: staff learn of events only by physical discovery or triggered alarm heard locally; no compliance log; no remote visibility |
| **Wireless IoT alarmed exit guards** ← Türwächter category | 46171600 | ~15% | Battery management overhead; wireless range limited in concrete (C3/C4); proprietary protocol (C6); no native integration with BACnet/KNX |
| **Security personnel stationed / patrolling exits** | — | <5% | High recurring labor cost; coverage gaps during breaks/shifts; human error; no automated log; scales poorly to multi-building campuses |
| **Do nothing / accept risk** | — | — | Zero P1 burden; compliance violation risk; insurance liability; state audit findings; zero monitoring of unauthorized entry through fire exits |

*"Do nothing" is the most common current state in budget-constrained districts: mechanical-only door closers with no alarm or monitoring.*

### 2.5 Scenarios and Output Type Activation

| Scenario | Output Types Activated | BOM/VN position | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Occupied-hours perimeter monitoring** — all secondary exits guarded during school day | All (elementary through charter) | L5.d.2 primary | Core scenario; applies universally; high school intensity highest (more doors, heavier traffic) |
| **After-hours / weekend unauthorized access** — sports facilities, evening events | Public middle and high; private | L5.d.2 + L6e integration | Secondary scenario; alarm-only mode; monitoring dashboard not staffed |
| **Emergency evacuation drill compliance** — documented evidence of door function | All | L5.d.2 + L6-H2 (compliance) | Generates compliance audit trail; tests alarm + key override |
| **Multi-building campus aggregation** — district-level dashboard | Multi-campus district segment | L5.d.2 × N buildings via gateway | IoT value driver: single dashboard across all buildings; activates receiver/gateway infrastructure |

*Sparsity check: After-hours scenario does not activate elementary schools as a distinct type (lower risk after hours). All other scenarios activate all output types uniformly. No dormant output types.*

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### 3.0 Stakeholder Roles

| Role | Who | Need Logic | Pyramid Levels |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Job Executor** | Campus security coordinator / head custodian | Operates monitoring daily; responds to alarms; manages battery changes | P1 ALL, P2, P3, P4, P5 |
| **Job Overseer** | School principal / district safety director | Accountable for state compliance; reviews dashboards; owns audit outcomes | P1 (own), P2, P4 |
| **Job Beneficiary** | Students and teachers | Receive the safety outcome; experience evacuation reliability | P2, P5 |
| **Lifecycle Support — Installer** | Security integrator / facilities crew | Installs, wires, configures, tests | P1 Preparation only |
| **Lifecycle Support — Maintainer** | Custodial / maintenance staff | Battery replacements, unit swaps | P1 Maintenance only |
| **Purchase Executor** | District procurement officer / school board | Makes financial decision | P1 Acquisition |
| **Purchase Influencer** | State safety auditor, fire marshal, insurance broker | Shapes compliance requirements; validates solutions | P2, P4 |

### 3.1 Burleson Pyramid — P1 by Alternative (Job Executor)

| Stage | Wired Panel | Mechanical-Only | Wireless IoT (Türwächter) | Security Personnel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acquire** | Write spec, issue RFP, qualify certified integrator, pull permit, schedule electricians | Buy from distributor; no spec required | Write spec, assess wireless coverage, plan receiver locations, issue RFP | Hire, background-check, assign post, integrate with front-office |
| **Prepare** | Route conduit to each door, wire alarm contacts, program fire panel zone, commission | Mount device per door; no conduit | Mount per door, place receivers, configure gateway, RF range-test | Brief/train, assign patrol routes, create incident log process |
| **Use** | Manage via fire alarm panel; respond to panel alerts; limited door-level granularity | No active monitoring; discover events physically or by local alarm | Monitor dashboard; respond to push alerts; distinguish event types | Rotate personnel; manage shift schedules; receive radio calls |
| **Maintain** | Annual panel inspection + device test; conduit repair if damaged | Annual mechanical test; replace battery if present | Replace batteries (schedule unknown; varies by door traffic); push firmware; test receiver links | HR: turnover, re-training, performance management |
| **Dispose** | Demolish conduit; decommission panel zone; integrate replacement into panel | Remove and discard unit | Swap unit; re-pair with receiver | Contract termination |

*Key insight: Wireless IoT has the lowest Preparation burden vs. wired (zero conduit). Maintenance burden higher than mechanical-only (battery management). "Do nothing" has zero P1 burden.*

### 3.2 Job Map — 8 Ulwick Steps

| # | Step | Statement (for this market) | Relevant? | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DEFINE** | Determine which exit doors require monitoring, what alarm parameters apply, and what coverage zone each receiver must serve | YES | Every deployment starts with a scoping decision; gaps here cause missed coverage |
| 2 | LOCATE | Locate available power sources, network drops, and structural paths for any infrastructure components | Partial | Wireless IoT minimizes this step, but receiver placement still requires assessment |
| 3 | **PREPARE** | Install door alarm units and infrastructure (receivers, gateways); configure system for monitoring | YES | Installation burden is the primary procurement barrier for aging buildings |
| 4 | **CONFIRM** | Verify all door units are functional before occupied school hours begin | YES | Daily pre-check; failure-to-confirm is where undetected device faults accumulate |
| 5 | **EXECUTE** | Monitor all exit doors in real time during occupied school hours; generate alerts on activation events | YES | Core function; primary safety value delivered here |
| 6 | **MONITOR** | Track device health (battery, signal strength, firmware); detect coverage drift | YES | Battery depletion is the dominant reliability failure mode for wireless IoT |
| 7 | **MODIFY** | Respond to alarm events; silence confirmed alarms; re-arm doors; update configuration | YES | False alarm management and re-arm speed determine operational acceptance |
| 8 | **CONCLUDE** | Log all door events; generate compliance reports; prepare audit evidence for fire marshal / state safety inspection | YES | Post-Uvalde compliance documentation is a named purchasing driver |

*Relevant steps: 7 of 8 (all except LOCATE are product-relevant). Highest opportunity: EXECUTE + MONITOR + CONCLUDE.*

### 3.3 Error Statements + ODI Scores

*Format: "Minimize the likelihood of [failure mode with ONE KPI]" / "Increase the ability to [capability with ONE KPI]". Solution-agnostic throughout.*

| # | Step | Error Type | Statement | Mkt-inherent? | Impact | I | S | OS | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E01 | DEFINE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of critical emergency exit points being omitted from the monitoring plan due to incomplete site assessment | ✓ | safety | 8 | 4 | 12 | Under |
| E02 | PREPARE | EFFORT | Minimize the total electrician labor hours required to install exit alarm monitoring systems at secondary and perimeter doors | ✓ | cost | 8 | 3 | 13 | Under |
| E03 | PREPARE | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize the likelihood that installation of exit alarm units requires structural modification to existing door frame or door hardware | ✓ | cost | 8 | 5 | 11 | — |
| E04 | PREPARE | EFFORT | Minimize the number of additional receiver and repeater infrastructure units required to achieve full wireless coverage across all exit points in a multi-story school building | tech-specific | cost | 7 | 4 | 10 | — |
| E05 | CONFIRM | EFFORT | Minimize the time required to verify that all exit alarm units are operational at the start of each school day | ✓ | time | 8 | 3 | 13 | Under |
| E06 | CONFIRM | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of device failures that remain undetected until an alarm event occurs during occupied school hours | ✓ | safety | 9 | 3 | 15 | Under |
| E07 | CONFIRM | EFFORT | Minimize the integration complexity required to connect exit door monitoring events to the district's existing access control or building management platform | tech-specific | cost | 7 | 3 | 11 | — |
| E08 | EXECUTE | DETECTION | Minimize the time elapsed between an unauthorized exit door activation and first staff notification during occupied school hours | ✓ | safety | 10 | 4 | 16 | Under |
| E09 | EXECUTE | CONSISTENCY | Minimize the frequency of false alarm events triggered by thermal door expansion, wind pressure, or incidental handle contact | ✓ | reliability | 8 | 4 | 12 | Under |
| E10 | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | Increase the ability to distinguish between a propped-open event, an alarm-triggered exit, and an authorized bypass activation on the monitoring dashboard | ✓ | safety | 8 | 3 | 13 | Under |
| E11 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize the degradation of alarm transmission coverage in building areas with reinforced concrete construction or metallic structural elements | tech-specific | reliability | 7 | 4 | 10 | — |
| E12 | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | Minimize the likelihood of missed alarm transmissions caused by radio frequency interference from co-located wireless networks in the school building | tech-specific | reliability | 7 | 5 | 9 | — |
| E13 | MONITOR | DETECTION | Minimize the number of monitoring coverage gaps caused by undetected battery depletion before door alarm operation is affected | tech-specific | reliability | 9 | 3 | 15 | Under |
| E14 | MONITOR | THROUGHPUT | Increase the ability to receive advance warning of battery status degradation at individual door units before monitoring capability is lost | tech-specific | reliability | 9 | 3 | 15 | Under |
| E15 | MONITOR | DETECTION | Minimize the time required to identify which specific door unit is experiencing a monitoring failure across a multi-building campus | ✓ | time | 8 | 3 | 13 | Under |
| E16 | MODIFY | EFFORT | Minimize the effort required to re-arm a door alarm unit after an authorized exit event without specialized tools or access credentials | ✓ | skill | 8 | 4 | 12 | Under |
| E17 | MODIFY | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of an alarm-silencing action occurring without generating a corresponding audit log entry for compliance records | ✓ | safety | 9 | 3 | 15 | Under |
| E18 | MODIFY | EFFORT | Minimize the effort required to register a replacement door alarm unit with the existing receiver network without installer software access | tech-specific | skill | 7 | 4 | 10 | — |
| E19 | CONCLUDE | EFFORT | Minimize the labor time required to compile door event logs into a format acceptable for state fire safety and school security audits | ✓ | cost | 8 | 2 | 14 | Under |
| E20 | CONCLUDE | ACCURACY | Minimize the likelihood of compliance audit findings related to gaps in exit monitoring documentation during annual fire marshal inspections | ✓ | safety | 9 | 3 | 15 | Under |
| E21 | CONCLUDE | THROUGHPUT | Increase the ability to present real-time exit monitoring compliance status to district administrators, school board members, or state inspectors without manual record assembly | ✓ | cost | 7 | 3 | 11 | — |
| E22 | P3 Role | — | Increase the ability to present auditable exit compliance records demonstrating that post-Uvalde legislative requirements were continuously met without incident | ✓ | safety | 8 | 3 | 13 | Under |
| E23 | P4 Image | — | Minimize the likelihood that an unsecured emergency exit is visible to media, parents, or state officials before campus staff have received notification [hypothesis] | ✓ | safety | 9 | 4 | 14 | Under |
| E24 | P5 Emotion | — | Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether all emergency exits in the building are currently secured during occupied school hours [hypothesis] | ✓ | safety | 8 | 3 | 13 | Under |

**Scores:** I = Importance (0-10). S = Satisfaction current (0-10). OS = opportunity score = I + max(I−S, 0). Under = Underserved (I>7 AND S<5).

**Yield check:**

| Metric | Target | Actual |
|---|---|---|
| Error statements | 15–30 | **24** ✓ |
| Needs per relevant step | ≥ 2 | Min 2 (DEFINE); Max 3 (EXECUTE, MONITOR) ✓ |
| Market-inherent (✓) | 60–70% | **17/24 = 71%** ✓ |
| Tech-specific (product_related) | 30–40% | **7/24 = 29%** ✓ |
| Underserved (I>7, S<5) | — | **16/24** |
| Top opportunity score | — | **E08: 16** |

### 3.4 P3–P5 Bridge Questions

| Level | Retrieval | Bridge | Resulting need |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P3 Role** | "By getting exit monitoring done perfectly, who does the facilities manager become?" → "The person who kept the school safe and passed the state audit without incident — the credible safety steward." | "What must the product do so they CAN be seen that way?" → Generate auditable, continuous compliance records without manual effort. | E22 |
| **P4 Image** | "Who is watching? What must they think?" → Parents, state auditors, media. Must think: "This district takes school safety seriously and can prove it." | "What must the product do so the watchers SEE that?" → Zero visible gaps: no propped door discovered before staff, no audit finding. | E23 |
| **P5 Emotion** | "What does the security coordinator want to FEEL?" → In control; certain every exit is secured. Want to AVOID: the anxiety of a propped fire exit that's been open for an hour without anyone knowing. | "What must the product do to produce that certainty?" → Real-time status; no silent gaps; immediate notification. | E24 |

### 3.5 Top Underserved Opportunities (OS ≥ 13)

| Rank | OS | Statement (short form) | Step | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 16 | Minimize detection time: unauthorized exit → staff notification | EXECUTE | safety |
| 2 | 15 | Minimize undetected device failures before school day | CONFIRM | safety |
| 2 | 15 | Minimize monitoring gaps from undetected battery depletion | MONITOR | reliability |
| 2 | 15 | Increase advance battery degradation warning | MONITOR | reliability |
| 2 | 15 | Minimize alarm silenced without audit log entry | MODIFY | safety |
| 2 | 15 | Minimize audit findings from documentation gaps | CONCLUDE | safety |
| 3 | 14 | Minimize labor to compile door event logs for audits | CONCLUDE | cost |
| 3 | 14 | Minimize likelihood unsecured exit visible to outsiders before staff notified | P4 | safety |

*The dominant opportunity cluster: monitoring visibility + compliance documentation. Türwächter's IoT layer directly addresses the top 8 underserved needs.*

---

sections.title: Validation Pass
sections.content:
| Check | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| V1 Format compliance | PASS | All 24 statements start "Minimize the likelihood of…" or "Increase the ability to…"; each contains exactly one measurable KPI |
| V2 product_related classification | PASS | 7 tech-specific needs would disappear if wired panel technology were chosen (battery, wireless range, protocol, repeater count, RF interference, re-pairing) |
| V3 Importance/satisfaction sanity | PASS | E08 = I:10 (life-safety, post-Uvalde regulatory mandate). No pattern of all-same scores |
| V4 Impact category accuracy | PASS | Detection-time = safety; documentation labor = cost; battery gaps = reliability; re-arm effort = skill |
| V5 Coverage | PASS | ≥2 per relevant step; 17/24 market-inherent (71%); no duplicates |
| V6 Solution-agnostic | PASS | Zero references to "GfS", "EasyWave", "868 MHz", "Türwächter", "piezo" in any error statement |
| V7 Anchor level | PASS | L5 (standalone system) — correct for "Emergency Exit Alarm & Monitoring Systems" which is a system, not a component |
| V8 Product job references parent | PASS | "Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to monitor and secure…" — parent unit named with context qualifier |

---

rows.CFJ: Deliver structured K-12 academic instruction to enrolled students within a safe, legally compliant, and supervised facility environment
rows.Output Types: Public elementary (K–5/K–6); Public middle (6–8); Public high school (9–12); Private/parochial (K–12 subset); Charter school (K–12 subset)
rows.Türwächter Offering Level: L5 — system within L6d process step. Route: PROCESS_VN (product enables the school's operation, never ends up in the educational output).

rows.Defining Circumstance: Average building age 49 years; no conduit to secondary exits; wiring retrofit cost-prohibitive
rows.Why Distinct: Wireless-only path; strong ROI on avoiding electrical work

rows.Defining Circumstance: School in 32+ US states with new door-security legislation; procurement driven by law not discretion
rows.Why Distinct: Safety budget unlocked; compliance deadline creates urgency

rows.Defining Circumstance: 10+ schools under single facilities director; centralized monitoring is the value driver, not per-door cost
rows.Why Distinct: Dashboard/aggregation value dominates; per-unit economics secondary

rows.Defining Circumstance: Schools with gyms, cafeterias, or sports fields accessed via secondary exits multiple times daily
rows.Why Distinct: Battery life constraint (C4) dominant; false alarm management critical

rows.Defining Circumstance: Independent procurement authority; can act without LEA approval cycle; often tighter budgets
rows.Why Distinct: Faster sales cycle; price sensitivity higher; IoT ROI must be self-evident

rows.Value: PROCESS_VN (product is used in campus operations; never part of educational output)
"Protect occupants and property while enabling legally compliant evacuation at all times"
Door Handle Guard, Alarm Horn/Sounder, Door Position Sensor, Wireless Radio Transmitter, Battery Pack, Wall-Mount Receiver/Gateway — all map 1:1 to Türwächter L4 subsystems
Monitor and secure (from L6d job_family: "protect occupants while enabling legally compliant evacuation")
Campus safety coordinator / facilities manager acting on behalf of L6d
"What monitoring and securing does Campus Safety & Emergency Management need to protect occupants and remain evacuation-compliant during occupied school hours?"

rows.Inherent Trade-offs: High installation labor; conduit to each door; retrofit disruption in 49-yr-old buildings; ongoing panel maintenance; certified integrator required
Zero monitoring: staff learn of events only by physical discovery or triggered alarm heard locally; no compliance log; no remote visibility

rows.Alternative: **Wireless IoT alarmed exit guards** ← Türwächter category
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Battery management overhead; wireless range limited in concrete (C3/C4); proprietary protocol (C6); no native integration with BACnet/KNX
High recurring labor cost; coverage gaps during breaks/shifts; human error; no automated log; scales poorly to multi-building campuses
Zero P1 burden; compliance violation risk; insurance liability; state audit findings; zero monitoring of unauthorized entry through fire exits

rows.Scenario: **Occupied-hours perimeter monitoring** — all secondary exits guarded during school day
rows.Notes: Core scenario; applies universally; high school intensity highest (more doors, heavier traffic)

rows.Scenario: **After-hours / weekend unauthorized access** — sports facilities, evening events
rows.Notes: Secondary scenario; alarm-only mode; monitoring dashboard not staffed

rows.Scenario: **Emergency evacuation drill compliance** — documented evidence of door function
rows.Notes: Generates compliance audit trail; tests alarm + key override
IoT value driver: single dashboard across all buildings; activates receiver/gateway infrastructure

rows.Need Logic: Operates monitoring daily; responds to alarms; manages battery changes
rows.Pyramid Levels: P1 ALL, P2, P3, P4, P5

rows.Need Logic: Accountable for state compliance; reviews dashboards; owns audit outcomes
Receive the safety outcome; experience evacuation reliability

rows.Who: State safety auditor, fire marshal, insurance broker

rows.Wired Panel: Write spec, issue RFP, qualify certified integrator, pull permit, schedule electricians
rows.Mechanical-Only: Buy from distributor; no spec required
rows.Wireless IoT (Türwächter): Write spec, assess wireless coverage, plan receiver locations, issue RFP
rows.Security Personnel: Hire, background-check, assign post, integrate with front-office

rows.Wired Panel: Route conduit to each door, wire alarm contacts, program fire panel zone, commission
rows.Mechanical-Only: Mount device per door; no conduit
rows.Wireless IoT (Türwächter): Mount per door, place receivers, configure gateway, RF range-test
rows.Security Personnel: Brief/train, assign patrol routes, create incident log process

rows.Wired Panel: Manage via fire alarm panel; respond to panel alerts; limited door-level granularity
rows.Mechanical-Only: No active monitoring; discover events physically or by local alarm
rows.Wireless IoT (Türwächter): Monitor dashboard; respond to push alerts; distinguish event types
rows.Security Personnel: Rotate personnel; manage shift schedules; receive radio calls

rows.Wired Panel: Annual panel inspection + device test; conduit repair if damaged
rows.Mechanical-Only: Annual mechanical test; replace battery if present
rows.Wireless IoT (Türwächter): Replace batteries (schedule unknown; varies by door traffic); push firmware; test receiver links

rows.Wired Panel: Demolish conduit; decommission panel zone; integrate replacement into panel

rows.Statement (for this market): Determine which exit doors require monitoring, what alarm parameters apply, and what coverage zone each receiver must serve
rows.Rationale: Every deployment starts with a scoping decision; gaps here cause missed coverage

rows.Statement (for this market): Locate available power sources, network drops, and structural paths for any infrastructure components
rows.Rationale: Wireless IoT minimizes this step, but receiver placement still requires assessment

rows.Statement (for this market): Install door alarm units and infrastructure (receivers, gateways); configure system for monitoring
rows.Rationale: Installation burden is the primary procurement barrier for aging buildings

rows.Statement (for this market): Verify all door units are functional before occupied school hours begin
rows.Rationale: Daily pre-check; failure-to-confirm is where undetected device faults accumulate

rows.Statement (for this market): Monitor all exit doors in real time during occupied school hours; generate alerts on activation events
rows.Rationale: Core function; primary safety value delivered here

rows.Statement (for this market): Track device health (battery, signal strength, firmware); detect coverage drift
rows.Rationale: Battery depletion is the dominant reliability failure mode for wireless IoT

rows.Statement (for this market): Respond to alarm events; silence confirmed alarms; re-arm doors; update configuration
rows.Rationale: False alarm management and re-arm speed determine operational acceptance

rows.Statement (for this market): Log all door events; generate compliance reports; prepare audit evidence for fire marshal / state safety inspection
rows.Rationale: Post-Uvalde compliance documentation is a named purchasing driver

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of critical emergency exit points being omitted from the monitoring plan due to incomplete site assessment
Minimize the total electrician labor hours required to install exit alarm monitoring systems at secondary and perimeter doors
Minimize the likelihood that installation of exit alarm units requires structural modification to existing door frame or door hardware
Minimize the number of additional receiver and repeater infrastructure units required to achieve full wireless coverage across all exit points in a multi-story school building
Minimize the time required to verify that all exit alarm units are operational at the start of each school day
Minimize the likelihood of device failures that remain undetected until an alarm event occurs during occupied school hours
Minimize the integration complexity required to connect exit door monitoring events to the district's existing access control or building management platform
Minimize the time elapsed between an unauthorized exit door activation and first staff notification during occupied school hours
Minimize the frequency of false alarm events triggered by thermal door expansion, wind pressure, or incidental handle contact
Increase the ability to distinguish between a propped-open event, an alarm-triggered exit, and an authorized bypass activation on the monitoring dashboard
Minimize the degradation of alarm transmission coverage in building areas with reinforced concrete construction or metallic structural elements
Minimize the likelihood of missed alarm transmissions caused by radio frequency interference from co-located wireless networks in the school building
Minimize the number of monitoring coverage gaps caused by undetected battery depletion before door alarm operation is affected
Increase the ability to receive advance warning of battery status degradation at individual door units before monitoring capability is lost
Minimize the time required to identify which specific door unit is experiencing a monitoring failure across a multi-building campus
Minimize the effort required to re-arm a door alarm unit after an authorized exit event without specialized tools or access credentials
Minimize the likelihood of an alarm-silencing action occurring without generating a corresponding audit log entry for compliance records
Minimize the effort required to register a replacement door alarm unit with the existing receiver network without installer software access
Minimize the labor time required to compile door event logs into a format acceptable for state fire safety and school security audits
Minimize the likelihood of compliance audit findings related to gaps in exit monitoring documentation during annual fire marshal inspections
Increase the ability to present real-time exit monitoring compliance status to district administrators, school board members, or state inspectors without manual record assembly
Increase the ability to present auditable exit compliance records demonstrating that post-Uvalde legislative requirements were continuously met without incident
Minimize the likelihood that an unsecured emergency exit is visible to media, parents, or state officials before campus staff have received notification [hypothesis]
Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether all emergency exits in the building are currently secured during occupied school hours [hypothesis]

rows.Retrieval: "By getting exit monitoring done perfectly, who does the facilities manager become?" → "The person who kept the school safe and passed the state audit without incident — the credible safety steward."
rows.Bridge: "What must the product do so they CAN be seen that way?" → Generate auditable, continuous compliance records without manual effort.

rows.Retrieval: "Who is watching? What must they think?" → Parents, state auditors, media. Must think: "This district takes school safety seriously and can prove it."
rows.Bridge: "What must the product do so the watchers SEE that?" → Zero visible gaps: no propped door discovered before staff, no audit finding.

rows.Retrieval: "What does the security coordinator want to FEEL?" → In control; certain every exit is secured. Want to AVOID: the anxiety of a propped fire exit that's been open for an hour without anyone knowing.
rows.Bridge: "What must the product do to produce that certainty?" → Real-time status; no silent gaps; immediate notification.

rows.Statement (short form): Minimize detection time: unauthorized exit → staff notification
Minimize undetected device failures before school day
Minimize monitoring gaps from undetected battery depletion
Minimize alarm silenced without audit log entry
Minimize audit findings from documentation gaps
Minimize labor to compile door event logs for audits
Minimize likelihood unsecured exit visible to outsiders before staff notified

rows.Notes: All 24 statements start "Minimize the likelihood of…" or "Increase the ability to…"; each contains exactly one measurable KPI
7 tech-specific needs would disappear if wired panel technology were chosen (battery, wireless range, protocol, repeater count, RF interference, re-pairing)
E08 = I:10 (life-safety, post-Uvalde regulatory mandate). No pattern of all-same scores
Detection-time = safety; documentation labor = cost; battery gaps = reliability; re-arm effort = skill
≥2 per relevant step; 17/24 market-inherent (71%); no duplicates
Zero references to "GfS", "EasyWave", "868 MHz", "Türwächter", "piezo" in any error statement
L5 (standalone system) — correct for "Emergency Exit Alarm & Monitoring Systems" which is a system, not a component
"Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to monitor and secure…" — parent unit named with context qualifier

job_map.product_job_statement: Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to monitor and secure all emergency exit doors against unauthorized use during occupied school hours while ensuring instant single-motion evacuation capability per IBC / NFPA 101 and generating auditable compliance documentation
job_map.cfj_statement: Deliver structured K-12 academic instruction to enrolled students within a safe, legally compliant, and supervised facility environment

job_steps.statement: Determine which exit doors require monitoring, what alarm parameters apply, and what coverage zone each receiver must serve
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Coverage gaps in the scoping decision propagate to unmonitored doors

job_steps.statement: Locate available infrastructure paths, power sources, and network access points for receiver and gateway placement
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Wireless IoT minimizes this step; receiver placement still requires assessment but is not the primary pain point

job_steps.statement: Install door alarm units and supporting infrastructure (receivers, gateways, repeaters); configure system for monitoring operation
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Installation burden is the primary procurement barrier for aging buildings

job_steps.statement: Verify all door alarm units are functional and monitoring is active before occupied school hours begin
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Daily pre-check; undetected device faults accumulate here

job_steps.statement: Monitor all designated exit doors in real time during occupied school hours; generate staff alerts on activation events
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Core safety function; primary value delivered here

job_steps.statement: Track device health (battery level, signal strength) and detect coverage drift before it causes monitoring failures
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Battery depletion is the dominant reliability failure mode for wireless IoT

job_steps.statement: Respond to alarm events; silence confirmed alarms; re-arm doors; update device configuration as needed
job_steps.relevance_rationale: False alarm management and re-arm speed determine operational acceptance

job_steps.statement: Log all door events; generate compliance documentation for fire marshal inspections and state school safety audits
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Post-Uvalde compliance documentation is a named purchasing driver in 32+ US states

incumbent_anchors.technology_name: Wired panic hardware with alarm panel integration
incumbent_anchors.rationale: Dominant installed base; wired to fire alarm panel; requires certified integrator and conduit; prohibitively expensive to retrofit in buildings with average age 49 years

incumbent_anchors.technology_name: Mechanical-only door alarms and contact alarms (no monitoring)
incumbent_anchors.rationale: Low-cost default; audible alarm only; zero remote monitoring; no compliance documentation; dominant in budget-constrained districts pre-Uvalde

incumbent_anchors.rationale: Fastest-growing segment; retrofit-friendly; battery-operated; Türwächter category. Primary vendors: GfS, Detex EAX series, dormakaba SafeRoute. Driven by inability to run conduit in aging building stock.
